title: Quiz: Dequantization and Classical Limits
description: Test your understanding of dequantization, quantum advantage limits, and when classical algorithms can match quantum performance

questions.question: What is 'dequantization' in the context of quantum algorithms?

options.text: Converting quantum computers back to classical
Finding classical algorithms that match quantum performance under the same assumptions
Reducing the number of qubits needed
Removing quantum noise from computations

questions.explanation: Dequantization means finding classical algorithms that achieve the same complexity as quantum algorithms by leveraging the same structural assumptions (like sampling access to data). Tang's work showed some quantum ML speedups could be matched classically.

questions.question: What does 'sample and query access' to a vector v provide?

options.text: Only reading individual entries v[i]
Query entries v[i], sample indices with probability |v[i]|^2/||v||^2, and access the norm
Random access to memory
Ability to modify vector entries

questions.explanation: Sample-query access provides: (1) O(1) query to any entry, (2) O(1) sampling of index i with probability proportional to |v[i]|^2, (3) O(1) access to ||v||. This is what quantum state preparation provides 'for free.'

questions.question: Why did Tang's algorithm dequantize quantum recommendation systems?

options.text: Quantum computers are fundamentally no better than classical
The quantum algorithm assumed sampling access, which classical can also provide
Recommendation systems are too simple for quantum advantage
The quantum algorithm had errors in its analysis

questions.explanation: The quantum recommendation algorithm assumed efficient sampling access to data. Tang showed that classical algorithms with the same sampling access (via data structures like KP-trees) can achieve similar performance, revealing the speedup came from the access model, not quantum mechanics.

questions.question: What is the 'data loading problem' for quantum linear algebra?

options.text: Quantum computers cannot store data
Loading classical data into quantum states typically requires O(n) operations
Data must be in binary format
Quantum memory is too small

questions.explanation: The data loading problem is that encoding an arbitrary n-dimensional classical vector into a quantum state generally requires O(n) operations. This can negate the exponential speedup claimed by algorithms like HHL, unless data has special structure or QRAM is available.

questions.question: Query complexity and time complexity are the same thing for quantum algorithms.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False! Query complexity counts oracle calls, while time complexity includes all operations. A quantum algorithm with O(log n) queries may still require O(n) time for state preparation, O(polylog n) gates per query, and O(n) time for output extraction.

questions.question: Which quantum application is considered 'robust' against dequantization?

options.text: Quantum recommendation systems
Quantum linear regression on classical data
Simulating quantum many-body systems
Quantum principal component analysis on classical matrices

questions.explanation: Simulating quantum many-body systems is robust because the problem itself is inherently quantum - there's no classical structure to exploit. The exponential state space of quantum systems requires quantum computers to simulate efficiently.

questions.question: When does HHL provide genuine quantum advantage?

options.text: Always, for any linear system
Never, due to dequantization
When the output feeds further quantum processing, avoiding O(n) readout
Only for 2x2 matrices

questions.explanation: HHL provides advantage when the quantum state |x> is used in subsequent quantum operations (like quantum ML pipelines), avoiding the O(n) cost of extracting the full classical solution. The intermediate quantum state stays in superposition.

questions.question: Which conditions support genuine quantum advantage? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Input data is already quantum (from quantum sensors/simulations)
Output feeds into further quantum processing
Problem has exponentially large state space
Classical data with no special structure

questions.explanation: Genuine quantum advantage is supported when: (a) quantum input avoids loading overhead, (b) quantum output avoids readout overhead, (c) exponential state space makes classical simulation intractable. Unstructured classical data (d) typically incurs O(n) loading costs.
